coredata.dc:title: The effect of charge on Zn tetra morpholine porphyrin conjugated to folic acid-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for photodynamic therapy studies
coredata.dc:creator: Magaela, N. Bridged
Matshitse, Refilwe
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description:
Zinc tetra morpholine porphyrin (complex 2), and its quaternized derivative (complex 3) were synthesized and conjugated to folic acid decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (FA-NGQDs) through
π − π
stacking to study their photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficacy. Photophysiochemical properties of complexes 2, 3, and their conjugates (2-FA-NGQDs, 3-FA-NGQDs) were studied. It was found that complex 3 had higher
ϕ
Δ
of 0.56 compared to complex 2 with
ϕ
Δ
of 0.24, and respective composites: 3-FA-NGQDs had higher
ϕ
Δ
compared to 2-FA-NGQDs. The PDT studies were conducted for nanoparticles (FA-NGQDs), complexes (2, 3), and respective composites (2-FA-NGQDs, and 3-FA-NGQDs) using MCF-7 breast cancer cell. Dark toxicity of all compounds was above 90% which is negligible. At a highest concentration of 40 µg/mL, 3-FA-NGQDs gave the lowest cell viability of 28% compared to all other conjugates and porphyrins alone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodynamic therapy
Folic acid
Graphene quantum dots
Morpholine porphyrin
Singlet oxygen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Metalation of tetra-4-morpholinyl porphyrin with zinc acetate dihydrate, complex 2, Scheme 1
Quaternization of zinc tetra-4-morpholinyl porphyrin to form complex 3, Scheme 1
Conjugation of complex 2 and 3 to folic acid decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots, Scheme 3
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs, Dynamic Scattering Light (DLS) & Zeta potential measurements
Singlet oxygen quantum yields and triplet lifetimes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representation of the ππ stacking for complex 2.

Uv-vis spectra of complexes 1, 2 and 3 in DMSO at 9
×
10
− 4
M
.
UV-vis spectra of NGQDs, FA-NGQDs, 2-FA-NGQDs, and 3-FA-NGQDs in DMSO.
TEM micrographs of FA-NGQDs and the conjugates, 2-FA-NGQDs, 3-FA-NGQDs.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) distribution for FA-NGQDs and conjugates.
FT-IR spectra of 2, 3, NGQDs, FA-NGQDs, 2-FA-NGQDs, and 3-FA-NGQDs.
EDS spectra of the FA-NGQDs and conjugates.
Raman spectra of FA-NGQDs and its conjugates.
Spectral changes observed during degradation of DMA in the presence of complex 2 (as an example) in DMSO.
Histograms showing (A) dark toxicity and (B) PDT activity of porphyrins and their conjugates.
MCF-7 cellular uptake over 24h at different concentration for porphyrins and their conjugates.
Nanoparticle, and nanoconjugates sizes and zeta potential values.

head.ce:title: The effect of charge on Zn tetra morpholine porphyrin conjugated to folic acid-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for photodynamic therapy studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Zinc tetra morpholine porphyrin (complex 2), and its quaternized derivative (complex 3) were synthesized and conjugated to folic acid decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (FA-NGQDs) through
π − π
stacking to study their photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficacy. Photophysiochemical properties of complexes 2, 3, and their conjugates (2-FA-NGQDs, 3-FA-NGQDs) were studied. It was found that complex 3 had higher
ϕ
Δ
of 0.56 compared to complex 2 with
ϕ
Δ
of 0.24, and respective composites: 3-FA-NGQDs had higher
ϕ
Δ
compared to 2-FA-NGQDs. The PDT studies were conducted for nanoparticles (FA-NGQDs), complexes (2, 3), and respective composites (2-FA-NGQDs, and 3-FA-NGQDs) using MCF-7 breast cancer cell. Dark toxicity of all compounds was above 90% which is negligible. At a highest concentration of 40 µg/mL, 3-FA-NGQDs gave the lowest cell viability of 28% compared to all other conjugates and porphyrins alone.

ce:section.ce:para:
Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a minimally invasive treatment modality for diseased cancer cells, which uses laser light of appropriate wavelength, a viable photosensitizer to generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS, including singlet oxygen) to eradicate diseased cells [1]. The development of novel photosensitizers that can improve the therapeutic outcome and reduce the non-specific side effects of the anticancer agents is of current interest in PDT research [2], hence in this work positively charged Zn porphyrin is conjugated to folic acid decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (FA-NGQDs) for application in PDT studies. Porphyrins are known photosensitizers for PDT [2–6]. Positively charged porphyrins can electrostatically interact with the negative charges present on the tumor cell membranes, facilitating penetration hence giving better results in PDT [4]. Metalation of porphyrins with diamagnetic metals (such as Zn2+ as employed in this work) is known to enhance the efficiency in intersystem crossing and thus improving singlet oxygen generation [5]. Folic acid (FA) is a cancer targeting agent, allowing for drug endocytosis via folate receptor (FR) [7], therefore decorating the photosensitizer with folic acid will improve selectivity [8]. Folic acid receptor is overexpressed in epithelial cancer cells including lung, brain, kidney, breast, and colon cancers. The properties of FR such as high binding affinity and small molecular size, enables it to deliver other molecules to cancer cells without causing harm to normal tissues [9]. Conjugation of folic acid to macromolecules (e.g. porphyrin) has been shown to enhance their delivery to folate receptor-expressing cancer cells [10]. Hence, in this study, positively charged and uncharged Zn porphyrins will be conjugated to NGQDs decorated with folic acid through
π − π
stacking, and PDT toxicity studies on MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines will be investigated. We have recently reported on a tetra morpholine porphyrin conjugated to nitrogen doped quantum dots for PDT studies [11], but the current study investigates for the first time positively charged Zn morpholine porphyrin conjugated to folic acid-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (FA-NGQDs). Porphyrins have been covalently linked to FA for PDT applications [12], in this work the porphyrins are combined with the conjugates of NGQDs and FA, for selectivity and targeting. The porphyrins used in this work contain morpholine which is a known anticancer agent, and morpholine has already been used as a component in a cancer drug called Gefitinib [13]. The potential of morpholine derivatives to increase potency and pharmacokinetics of cancer drugs has been demonstrated [13].

The porphyrins employed are the neutral (2) and quaternized (3) tetra-4-morpholinyl porphyrins, Scheme 1 . The starting free base complex 1 and the Sn derivatives are known [11], but complexes 2 and 3 are reported for the first time.

1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylamonopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), folic acid, quinine sulfate, zinc acetate dihydrate, deuterated chloroform (CDCl3), dimethyl formamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfate, chloroform, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), Zn 5,10,15,20-tetraphenyl-21H,23H-porphine (ZnTPP), 9,10 dimethylanthracene (DMA), LUDOX HS-40 colloidal silica, and alpha-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid, were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The synthesis of NQGDs [14] (using citric acid and urea, hence containing NH2 groups) has been reported. Highly purified water was obtained from the installed purifying system from ELGA, Veolia water PURELAB, Flex system (Marlow, UK). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10 mM) pH 7.4 was prepared using appropriate amounts of Na2HPO4 and NaOH.

Human breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7 were purchased from Cellonex®. Dulbecco's phosphate-buffer saline, Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM, containing 4.5 g/ L-glucose with L-glutamine) with and without phenyl-red were purchased from Lonza®. The heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10% (v/v)) and the 100 μg/ mL-penicillin:100 unit/ mL-streptomycin-amphotericin-B-mixture (PSA) as well as the neutral red cell proliferation reagent (WST) were all purchased from Biowest®.

Ultraviolet visible (UV-vis) optical absorption spectroscopy was recorded using Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer, mass spectral analysis was performed using Brucker AutoFLEX III smartbeam TOF/TOF Mass spectrometer using a positive ion mode using alpha-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid as a MALDI matrix. Fluorescence emission spectra was recorded using Varian Eclipse spectrofluorometer, transmission electron microscope (TEM) pictures were recorded using JOEL JEM 1210cat 100kV acceleration voltage, infra-red spectra were recorded using a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR spectrometer with a platinum-ATR, proton nuclear magnetic resonance (¹H NMR) spectra were recorded using Bruker EMX600 MHz NMR spectrometer, dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to measure particle size distribution of nanoparticles and nanoconjugates using a Malvern Zetasizer Nanoscience. Nano-ZS90, Merck Eppendorf centrifuge 5810 was used for precipitates extraction. Merck Eppendorf centrifuge 5810 used for extraction. For phase identification of the materials, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using Bruker D8, with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å, nickel filter).

The triplet lifetimes (τT) were obtained using a laser flash photolysis system consisting of an LP980 spectrometer with a PMT-LP detector and an ICCD camera (Andor DH320T-25F03). The signal from a PMT detector was recorded on a Tektronix TDS3012C digital storage oscilloscope. The excitation pulses were produced using a tunable laser system consisting of an Nd:YAG laser (355 nm, 135 mJ/ 4–6 ns) pumping an optical parametric oscillator (OPO, 30 mJ/3–5 ns) with a wavelength range of 420–2300 nm (NT-342B,Ekspla). The data were determined by exponential fitting of the kinetic curve using Origin Pro 8 software. Photo-irradiations for singlet oxygen studies were done using the laser system described above. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time-correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 300, PicoquantR GmbH) with a diode laser (LDH-P-670, Isoquants GmbH,20 MHz repetition rate, 44 ps pulse width) [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metalation of tetra-4-morpholinyl porphyrin with zinc acetate dihydrate, complex 2, Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para:
In a 100 mL round bottom flask containing chloroform (10 mL), complex 1 (0.8 g, 0.00083 mol) was added followed by excess amount of zinc acetate dihydrate (2.4 g, 0.010 mol) in MeOH (8 mL). The mixture was allowed to reflux for 2 h, then, the metalated crude was poured into water (100 mL) and the organic layer was collected and dried followed by the purification with aluminium oxide neutral column chromatography with chloroform as eluent to obtain pure complex 2. Yield: 70%. UV/Vis (DMSO)
λ
m a x
nm (log
ε
): 432 (4.14), 563 (3.42), 610 (3.32). FTIR (cm −1) 3076 (Ar,C-H), 1546 (Ar,C=N), 1413 (C-N), 1147 (C-O-C). 1H NMR (CDCI3):
δ
ppm 7.47 (s, 4H, Ar, pyrrole), 7.29 (d, 4H, J= 2.0 Hz, Ar, pyrrole), 7.09 (d, 8H, J = 3.4 Hz Ar, phenyl), 7.05 (d, 8H, J = 2.5 Hz, Ar, phenyl), 2.65 (m, J = 3.4 Hz, 16H, CH2), 1.20 (m, J = 3.4 Hz,16H, CH2). MALDI-TOF MS. Calc 1016.37, found 1017.92 [M+H] +.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quaternization of zinc tetra-4-morpholinyl porphyrin to form complex 3, Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para:
Complex 2 (0.3 g, 0.00023 mol) was dissolved in 5 mL of DMF, followed by the addition of dimethyl sulfate (3 mL). The mixture was allowed to reflux for 30 min,then, the compound was centrifuged and washed with EtOH to remove excess DMF and the unquaternized species, complex 3, was then obtained. Yield: 62%. UV/Vis (DMSO)
λ
m a x
nm (log
ε
): 436 (4.36), 568 (3.72), 612(3.51) FTIR (cm1) 3419 (OH), 3054 (Ar, C-H), 1657 (Ar, C=N), 1446 (C-N). 1H NMR (DMF):
δ
ppm 8.30 (s,4H, Ar, pyrrole), 8.05 (s, 4H, Ar, pyrrole) 7.80 (d, 8H, J = 1.7 Hz, Ar, phenyl) 7.70 (d, 8H, J = 5.1 Hz, Ar, phenyl), 4.01 (s, 3H, CH3O), 3.25 (m, 16H, J = 10.1 Hz, CH2), 2.85 (s, 12H, quaternized methyl-H), 2.40 (m, 16H, J = 10.2 Hz, CH2).

Folic acid (40 mg) and PBS (30 mL) were added to a beaker, and the mixture was stirred for 2 h at room temperature to allow folic acid to dissolve. Then, EDC (0.14 g, 0.0009 mol) and NHS (0.10g, 0.0009 mol) were added under magnetic stirring in the dark for 24 h. After the reaction had completed, NGQDs were added to the solution followed by further stirring for 24 h. Finally, dialysis was carried out for one day using a dialysis membrane and freeze drying was carried out to obtain folic acid-conjugated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (FA-NGQDs) [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of complex 2 and 3 to folic acid decorated nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots, Scheme 3
ce:section.ce:para: Complex 2 (0.1 g, 0.00009 mol) and 3 (0.1 g 0.00092 mol) were dissolved separately in 10 mL of DMF, then FA-NGQDs (0.8 g) were added to the mixtures followed by stirring in the dark at room temperature for 48 h. The products obtained were centrifuged and washed with EtOH to unreacted species

Complex 2 was synthesized by insertion of Zn in the known complex 1, Scheme 1. Then complex 3 was formed by quaternization of 2. The molecular peak ion was observed at m/z = 1017.93 for complex 2 that is [M+H]+of what was expected, Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). Complex 3 did not ionize so there is no mass spectrum. 1H NMR spectrum for complex 2 showed 56H as expected, Fig. S2 Supporting Information. For complex 3 the number of protons obtained were 71H as expected Fig. S2. Folic acid was linked to NGQDs (to form FA-NGQDs) using EDC/NHS coupling to form an amide bond, Scheme 2 . Then the porphyrins were assembled onto FA-NGQDs by π-π stacking as shown in Scheme 3 .

Uv-vis spectra in Fig. 2 of the NGQDs showed that they absorb at 337 nm, and upon conjugation of NGQDs to the folic acid (FA-NGQDs) the peak shifted to 356 nm, Table 1. This red shift is caused by an increase in sp2 C (C-C/C=C) from the folic acid. The Soret band of complex 2-FA-NGQDs was observed at 437 nm resulting in a red shift by 5 nm compared to complex 2 alone, and red shifts of Soret bands are often observed for distorted porphyrins [18]. In addition, red shifts are associated with J aggregation in porphyrins [19]. Complex 3-FA-NGQDs, the Soret band is blue shift by 17 nm compared to complex 3 alone, and blue shift suggest strong interaction between complex 3 and FA-NGQDs [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs, Dynamic Scattering Light (DLS) & Zeta potential measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Different properties of nanoparticles and nanoconjugates such as size, polydispersity index (PDI), and zeta potential play an important role in their physicochemical and biological properties [21]. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to determine the average particle distribution. The average diameters in Fig. 4 are 15.71, 24.31, and 32.20 nm for FA-NGQDs, 2-FA-NGQDs, and 3-FA-NGQDs, respectively, Table 2. The increase in size of the conjugates compared to NGQDs alone shows the formation of supramolecular assemblies of NGQDs and the porphyrin. Discrepancies in particle size determined by TEM and DLS could be attributed to interference of the dispersant into hydrodynamic diameter, resulting in higher values for DLS [22]. The zeta potential values are: FA-NGQDs (-18.5 mV), 2-FA-NGQDs (-13.5 mV), 3-FA-NGQDs (7.61 mV), Table 2. The positive zeta potential indicates that the dispersed particles in a suspension are positively charged whereas the negative zeta potential indicates that the dispersed particles in the suspension are negatively charged, hence 3-FA-NGQDs has a positive value since complex 3 is positively charged. Lower zeta potentials values (regardless of charge) suggest interactions among the nanoparticles, forming fractal aggregates [23], and the higher zeta potential value indicates long term stability, good colloidal nature, and low aggregation tendency of the nanoparticles [24–26]. Thus, FA-NGQDs are more stable than NGQDs and the stability of the former is reduced in the presence of porphyrins. Poly dispersity index (PDI) which is representation of the distribution of size populations within a given sample was determined using DLS [27], and the PDI values obtained for NGQDs, FA-NGQDs, 2-FA-NGQDs, and 3-FA-NGQDs were 1.00, 0.92, 0.64 and 0.49, respectively. The values of conjugates signify monomodal dispersions while the values of NGQDs and FA-NGQDs signifies broad particle size distribution.

FT-IR was used to confirm the functional groups present in the compounds. The successful covalent linking of folic acid to NGQDs was confirmed by an amide bond at 1657 cm−1. The spectra of NGQDs alone showed OH peak at 3308 cm−1 and C-N at 1558 cm−1, but upon covalently linkage with FA the OH became more intense and amide bond was observed. Upon
π − π
stacking of complex 2 or 3 on to FA-NGQDs there are vibrational peak shifts suggesting presence of molecular interactions [28].

EDX was used to determine the elemental composition of FA-NGQDs, complexes 2 in comparison to 3 and of the conjugates, Fig. 6
. In complex 2 the presence of Zn was observed with other expected elements, and for complex 3 there was presence of all elements observed in 2 and sulfur which is from the quaternizing reagent. It was observed that both the elements observed in FA-NGQDs alone and 2 were observed in 2-FA-NGQDs. All the expected elements in 3-FA-NGQDs were observed.

The Raman spectra in Fig. 7 exhibit diagnostic Raman bands termed the G and D bands resulting from the E2g tangential vibrational mode of the sp2 bonded carbons and the disordered A1g breathing vibrational mode of the aromatic sp3 carbon rings, respectively [29].

The disorder (D, sp3) and graphitic (G, sp2) bands were observed, respectively at (1046,1423 cm−1) for FA-NGQDs, (1045, 1421 cm−1) for 2-FA-NGQDs and (1043,1420 cm−1) for 3-FA-NGQDs, the shifts in the D and G band are an indication of strong interaction between porphyrins and graphitic sheets due to a charge transfer [30]. The ID/IG ratio of FA-NGQDs, 2-FA-NGQDs and 3-FA-NGQDs were 0.73, 0.85, and 0.51, respectively while the ratio for NGQDs was reported as 0.86 [11]. The ratio for FA-NGQDs is surprisingly lower than for NGQDs. The decrease could be due to the additional sp2 carbons from folic acid in FA-NGQDs compared to NGQDs. It was also not expected for 2-FA-NGQDs to result in higher ID/IG ratio compared to FA-NGQDs, since it is generally expected that
π − π
conjugation will not affect the NGQDs structure as much as covalent bonding. Increases or decreases in ID:IG ratios of GQDs have been previously reported to be due to size-dependent edge-state variations of GQDs [31].

Following the comparative methods reported in literature [32], fluorescence quantum yield ( ϕ
F) were obtained using ZnTPP as a standard ( ϕ
F std = 0.033) [33] in DMF for porphyrins complexes, and quinine sulfate in 0.05 M in H2SO4 ϕ F = 0.52 [34]) was employed as a standard for NGQDs and FA-NGQDs (in the absence of porphyrins). NGQDs and FA-NGQDs were excited at 420 nm and the porphyrins and conjugates at 430 nm. The ϕ F value obtained for FA-NGQDs was 0.04, lower than for NGQDs at 0.70, suggesting FA quenches fluorescence of NGQDs (with the emission spectra shown in Fig. S3. For complexes 2,3 and the conjugates (2-FA-NGQDs, 3-FA-NGQDs), the ϕ F values obtained were (0.08, 0.03, 0.05 and < 0.01), respectively and these values are low typical of metalloporphyrins [33] and also the heavy atom effect of sulfur in complex 3 and 3-FA-NGQDs. It is important to note that exciting the conjugate may excite both porphyrins and NGQDs. The fluorescence life-times ( τ
F) of porphyrin complexes and conjugates were studied using TCSPC analysis by excitation at emission maxima. The fluorescence lifetimes values obtained were low corresponding to low ϕ F values, and the values obtained were (0.58, 1.77, 1.28, 1.39, and 0.001) for FA-NGQDs, 2, 3, 2-FA-NGQDs, and 3-FA-NGQDs, respectively, following the trend in ϕ F values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Singlet oxygen quantum yields and triplet lifetimes
ce:section.ce:para:
Singlet oxygen quantum yield (
ϕ
Δ
) of all the porphyrin complexes and conjugates was determined using DMA as a singlet quencher and ZnTPP (
ϕ
Δ
std = 0.53) as a standard in DMSO [35]. The values of
ϕ
Δ
were calculated using the equation reported in literature [36]. The decrease of DMA peaks was monitored at intervals for 80 s, Fig. 8 (using complex 2 as an example). Complex 2 (
ϕ
Δ
= 0.24) in Table 2 had a low singlet oxygen quantum yield value compared to 3 (
ϕ
Δ
=0.56), and this is due to the presence sulfur atom in 3 which enhance the rate of intersystem crossing to the triplet state. Dyes with sulfur are reported to have internal heavy atom effect [37], and they show larger increase in the rate of intersystem crossing which leads to higher oxygen yield [38]. Conjugates 2-FA-NGQDs and 3-FA-NGQDs had higher singlet quantum yields (
ϕ
Δ
= 0.97) and (
ϕ
Δ
= 0.99), respectively. Thus, FA improves singlet oxygen quantum yields of porphyrins as has been observed before [12]. The triplet lifetime (τT) for 3-FA-NGQDs is longer than for 3 alone, the same applies for complex 2 and its conjugate, the triplet decay curve (for complex 3 as an example) is shown in Fig. S4. Thus the FA-NGQDs improves the triplet lifetime for porphyrins, hence the improved singlet oxygen quantum yield of porphyrins in the presence of FA-NGQDs, since singlet oxygen is generated from the triplet state.

The MCF-7 cells were grown to 100% confluency, and the stock concentration of all complexes was prepared by dissolving each complex (5 mg) in 1% DMSO and making the volume up with supplemented media. DMSO (1%) used had a negligible effect on the cells (Fig. S5).

The dark cytotoxicity studies of all complexes, nanoparticles and their conjugates were performed at concentrations ranging from 2.5 to 40 µg/mL, and at all the concentrations the cell viability percentages of the complexes and their conjugates were above 90%, Fig. 9 A. Thus, suggesting that the photosensitizers are not harmful to MCF-7 breast cancer cells in the absence of irradiation. In vitro dark cytotoxicity is undesirable for photosensitizers aimed for use in PDT. The dark toxicity of NGQDs was previously reported and cell viabilities were also > 90% [11].

The MCF-7 cancer cells were treated with same range of concentrations (2.5−40 µg/mL) that was used for dark cytotoxicity studies as shown in Fig. 9B. The cells were irradiated with Thorlabs M625L3 LED (625 nm) for 30 min. 3-FA-NGQDs exhibited higher PDT effect with cell viability of 28% at 40 µg/mL compared to 3 alone which exhibited cell viability of 37%, this suggest that the FA-NGQDs enhanced the PDT effect of complex 3. The same trend was observed with 2-FA-NGQDs when is compared to 2 alone, where 2-FA-NGQDs exhibited cell viability 31% and complex 2 exhibited cell viability of 45% also suggesting that FA-NGQDs improved the PDT effect of complex 2. Conjugates performed better as expected because the folic acid on the NGQDs has high selectivity, and nanoparticles are reported to enhance the delivery of the photosensitizer dye and selective accumulation in cancer tumors due to enhanced solubility in aqueous solvents and the enhanced permeability and retention effect [39]. Complex 3 showed higher PDT effect when compared to complex 2, and this is due to the cationic charge on 3 because positively charged porphyrins can penetrate the tumor cells membranes easily giving better PDT results [4].

The cellular uptake plays an important role in the PDT activity of the photosensitizer. The cellular uptake was determined by measuring absorbance of internalized photosensitizer drug after incubating with MCF-7 cells for 24h. At higher concentrations, 2-FA-NGQDs had higher cellular uptake compared to 2 alone as shown in Fig. 10 . The same applies to 3-FA-NGQDs compared to 3. Therefore, it can be concluded that FA-NGQDs improved the uptake efficacy of 2 and 3 due to the tumor targeting properties of folic acid and NGQDs [16]. 3-FA-NGQDs at had the highest cellular uptake at higher concentrations and this is attributed by cationic charges and high selectivity from FA-NGQDs which makes it to easily penetrate the cancer cells.

Complexes 2 (neutral) and 3 (cationic) were synthesized. The photophysicochemical studies were performed these complexes, and it was found that 3 had higher singlet oxygen quantum yield compared to 2 alone. These complexes were also conjugated to FA-NGQDs to improve their PDT efficacy and the conjugates were characterized using different techniques. Cellular uptake studies were conducted, and it was observed that the conjugates (2-FA-NGQDs and 3-FA-NGQDs) exhibited higher cellular uptake compared to the corresponding 2 and 3 alone. The conjugates had higher PDT activity against MCF-7 cells.

N. Bridged Magaela: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Refilwe Matshitse: Validation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Tebello Nyokong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) Innovation and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DSI/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University, and DSI/Mintek Nanotechnology Innovation Centre.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.pdpdt.2022.102898.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photo-triggered release of doxorubicin from liposomes formulated by amphiphilic phthalocyanines for combination therapy to enhance antitumor efficacy
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Zheng, H. Liu, X. Liu, L. Jiang, L. Li, X. Wu, N. Guo, C. Ding and M. Huang. Photo-triggered release of doxorubicin from liposomes formulated by amphiphilic phthalocyanines for combination therapy to enhance antitumor efficacy. J. Mater. Chem. B8(2020) 8022-8036.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cationic porphyrin derivatives for application in photodynamic therapy of cancer
Laser Phys.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.P.P. McCormick, M.F Pansa, L.N.M. Sanabria, C.M.B. Carvalho, M.A.F. Faustino, M.G.P.M.S. Neves, J.A.S. Cavaleiro, N.B.R. Vittar, V.A. Rivarola. Cationic porphyrin derivatives for application in photodynamic therapy of cancer. Laser Phys.24(2014)045603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photodynamic therapy for cancer and for infections: what is the difference?
Isr. J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Sharma, P. Mroz, T. Dai, Y.Y Huang, T.G.S. Denis, M.R. Hamblin. Photodynamic therapy for cancer and for infections: What is the difference?Isr. J. Chem.52(2012) 691–705.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of overall charge and charge distribution on cellular uptake, distribution and phototoxicity of cationic porphyrins in HEp2 cells
J. Photochem. Photobiol. B Biol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.J. Jensen, M.G.H. Vicente, R. Luguya, J. Norton, F.R. Fronczek, K.M. Smith. Effect of overall charge and charge distribution on cellular uptake, distribution and phototoxicity of cationic porphyrins in HEp2 cells. J. Photochem. Photobiol. B Biol.100(2010) 100–111.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal atom effect on the photophysical properties of Mg (II), Zn (II), Cd (II), and Pd (II) tetraphenylporphyrin complexes proposed as possible drugs in photodynamic therapy
Molecules

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-aggregated lipophilic water-soluble tin porphyrins as photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy and photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy
